coredata.dc:title: Improved comprehensive performance of CsPbI2Br perovskite solar cells by modifying the photoactive layers with carbon nanodots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 The Chinese Ceramic Society. Production and hosting by Elsevier B.V.

Xie, Caidong
Yao, Xincheng
Chen, Qiulu
Liu, Weining

Liu, Qiming

Li, Yali
He, Deyan
coredata.dc:description: Introducing additives into perovskite layers is an effective method to enhance the power conversion efficiency (PCE) and operation stability of perovskite solar cells (PSCs). Herein, we reported an addition of carbon nanodots (CNDs) into the CsPbI2Br photoactive layer to boost performance of the related PSCs. It is found that the trap density can be notably suppressed, and the crystallinity can be enhanced after introducing CNDs with an optimal quantity. The PSC with 1.0 wt% addition of CNDs delivers a notable improved average PCE of 13.77% (the highest PCE: 14.69%) from that of 12.14% for the control device without CND addition. Moreover, the CND-added CsPbI2Br PSCs exhibit superior stability, i.e., ∼86 % retention of the initial PCE after 160 h aging in air with the humidity of 20%–30%, to the control device.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CsPbI2Br perovskite Solar cells
Carbon nanodots
Power conversion efficiency
Stability

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns of the CsPbI2Br films with varying CND addition. (b) Comparison of the (100) peaks of the pristine and 1.0 wt% CND-added CsPbI2Br films. (c) Absorption spectra and (d) Tauc plot of the CsPbI2Br films with varying CND addition.
Top-view SEM (left) and AFM (right) images of (a, a′) the pristine and (b, b′) 0.5 wt% and (c, c′) 1.0 wt% and (d, d′) 1.5 wt% CND-added CsPbI2Br films on ITO glass.
Cross-sectional SEM images of (a) the pristine and (b) 1.0 wt% CND-added CsPbI2Br films on ITO glass.

(a) Schematized device configuration, (b) cross-sectional SEM image and (c) energy level diagram of each component for the CsPbI2Br solar cells investigated in this study. (d) J-V curves of the devices with varying CND addition at AM 1.5G. (e) Statistical chart of the PCE values and (f) time-dependent J for the devices with and without 1.0 wt% CND addition.

(a) Steady-state PL spectra of the pristine and 1.0 w t% CND-added CsPbI2Br on SnO2-coated ITO glass. (b) Space-charge-limited current versus applied voltage of the devices (ITO/SnO2/CsPbI2Br/PCBM/Ag) without and with 1.0 wt% CND addition in the CsPbI2Br layer. (c) Nyquist plots at the bias of 1.0 V (the inset depicts the equivalent circuit mode for CsPbI2Br PSCs in EIS). (d) R rec curves of the pristine and 1.0 wt% CNDs-doped CsPbI2Br PSCs based different applied voltage.

(a) Test of performance stability for the pristine and 1.0 wt% CND-added PSCs in air with the humidity of 20–30 %. (b) and (c) show the contact-angle test of the pristine and 1.0 wt% CND-added CsPbI2Br layers, respectively.
Summary of the key device parameters extracted from the J-V curves shown in Fig. 4(d).

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: Peer review under responsibility of The Chinese Ceramic Society.

head.ce:title: Improved comprehensive performance of CsPbI2Br perovskite solar cells by modifying the photoactive layers with carbon nanodots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Special Function Materials & Structure Design of the Ministry of Education, And School of Materials & Energy, Lanzhou University, 222 South Tianshui Road, Lanzhou, 730000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Special Function Materials & Structure Design of the Ministry of Education

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Special Function Materials & Structure Design of the Ministry of Education, And School of Materials & Energy, Lanzhou University, 222 South Tianshui Road, Lanzhou, 730000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Introducing additives into perovskite layers is an effective method to enhance the power conversion efficiency (PCE) and operation stability of perovskite solar cells (PSCs). Herein, we reported an addition of carbon nanodots (CNDs) into the CsPbI2Br photoactive layer to boost performance of the related PSCs. It is found that the trap density can be notably suppressed, and the crystallinity can be enhanced after introducing CNDs with an optimal quantity. The PSC with 1.0 wt% addition of CNDs delivers a notable improved average PCE of 13.77% (the highest PCE: 14.69%) from that of 12.14% for the control device without CND addition. Moreover, the CND-added CsPbI2Br PSCs exhibit superior stability, i.e., ∼86 % retention of the initial PCE after 160 h aging in air with the humidity of 20%–30%, to the control device.

ce:section.ce:para: Organic-inorganic hybrid perovskite solar cells (PSCs) have attracted tremendous attention owing to the excellent comprehensive optoelectronic properties, such as high absorption coefficients, long electron-hole diffusion lengths and tunable bandgaps for the related photoactive materials [1–6]. The power conversion efficiency (PCE) of the organic-inorganic hybrid PSCs has risen unprecedentedly to 25.5 % from 3.8 % since the first report in 2009 [7,8]. Moreover, facile fabrication compatible with the solution processes enables notable reduction of the fabrication cost as compared to other photovoltaic technologies such as crystalline silicon solar cells. However, due to the volatile and hygroscopic organic cations of CH3NH3 + and CH (NH2)2 + in the organic-inorganic perovskites, the inherent structural instability would seriously influence the operation stability for this promising solar cell technology [9].

As a unique alternative, all-inorganic perovskites, substituting the organic cations by the Cs cation, show better light, moisture and especially thermal stability [10–13]. Among Cs-based all-inorganic perovskites, CsPbI3 in the cubic crystal structure exhibits the sufficient absorption of visible light because of the optical bandgap of 1.73 eV [14,15]. Nevertheless, CsPbI3 PSCs suffer from the instable performance due to the severe octahedral rotation distortion and spontaneous transformation from α-phase to the undesired δ-phase at room temperature [16]. Mixed-halide CsPbI2Br with a suitable bandgap of ∼1.9 eV may balance light harvesting and phase stability [17]. For CsPbI2Br-based PSCs, the primary issue needing to be resolved is the inferior PCE to that of the typical hybrid perovskite counterparts [18]. Many efforts have thus been paid on the issues including energy level matching and defect passivation for high-performance CsPbI2Br PSCs with the minimized energy loss [19–23]. Among them, passivating defects is critical for achieving a high carrier mobility, long carrier lifetime and diffusion length [24,25]. Additive engineering has proven to be an effective method for passivating the grain boundaries, improving the crystallinity, and thus promoting optoelectronic properties of the perovskite layers [26,27]. To date, additives such as salts, molecules and polymers have been applied for CsPbI2Br PSCs [28–34].

Carbon nanodots (CNDs), as an important member of the carbon material family, have drawn a lot of attention because of the unique physical and chemical properties [35,36]. Recently, an investigation regarding performance enhancement for inverted CH3NH3PbI3 hybrid PSCs by grain boundary passivation using CNDs has been reported [37]. Nevertheless, report on modification of the CsPbI2Br layers with CNDs to improve performance of the related PSC2s has been rarely found. In this report, addition of CNDs into the CsPbI2Br layers to modify the physical and chemical properties and thus to improve the performance of the CsPbI2Br PSCs is discussed. The results indicate that the CND addition with an appropriate quantity can enhance the crystal quality, reduce the defect density, and improve the electrical properties and structural stability of the CsPbI2Br layers. As a result, the PSCs with 1.0 wt% addition of CNDs deliver the averaged PCE of 13.77 % with an evident increase from 12.14 % for the control device without CND addition. Moreover, the enhanced operation stability is observed for the CND-added devices.

CNDs were fabricated by a microwave-assisted method [38]. 1 g citric acid and 2 g urea were first dispersed in 20 mL deionized (DI) water. Then, the mixture solution was placed in a microwave oven for 5 min until obtaining a dark cluster that was then dried at 60 °C for 1 h. Following that, the product was dissolved in ethanol and centrifugated at 8000 rpm/min. After collecting the solid product, it was dried at 60 °C to obtain CNDs.

Etched ITO glass substrates were first ultrasonically cleaned in the sequence of ethanol, acetone and isopropanol each for 15 min. After drying, the ITO surface was treated by air plasma for 2.5 min to make a hydrophilic surface. Then, the SnO2 colloid precursor (15 wt% in H2O colloidal dispersion) was diluted in DI water with a volume ratio of 1:4, followed by spin-coating onto the ITO substrates at 3000 rpm for 40 s and annealing on a hot plate at 150 °C for 30 min in air to form the SnO2 electron transport layer. For preparation of the CsPbI2Br layers, CsI, PbI2 and PbBr were stoichiometrically dissolved in a mixed solvent of DMF and DMSO with a volume ratio of 4:1 to form a 1 M solution. Then CNDs were added into the 1 M CsPbI2Br precursor solution and stirred at 90 °C for 1 h. The precursor solution was then spin-coated onto the SnO2-coated ITO glass substrates at 3000 rpm for 60 s inside an argon-filled glovebox, which was followed by in situ drying firstly at 50 °C for 1 min and then at 160 °C for 10 min to form the CND-embedded CsPbI2Br layers. The 16 mg mL−1 P3HT solution was then spin-coated on top of the CsPbI2Br layer at 2000 rpm for 30 s and baked at 100 °C for 5 min. Finally, Ag strips were thermally evaporated on top of the P3HT hole transport layer (HTL) to form the anode electrode.

The morphology of the samples was characterized by a scanning electron microscope (SEM, MIRA3, TESCAN) and atomic force microscope (AFM, NT-MDT). The structural and compositional information was checked by an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Malvern Panalytical Empyrean with Cu K
α radiation), transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F30), and micro-Raman spectrometer (Jobin-Yvon Horiba HR800 with the excitation wavelength of 532 nm). Optical absorption spectra were measured using a UV-VIS-NIR spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV-3600). Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded by a RF-5301PC spectrometer (Shimadzu) with the excitation wavelength of 525 nm. The current density-voltage (J-V) characteristic curves were recorded using a Keithley 2400 source meter at air mass (AM) 1.5G illumination (100 mW cm−2). The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) test was performed in a frequency range of 1–106 Hz at an AC voltage with an amplitude of 10 mV using the CHI660E electrochemical workstation.

The XRD pattern and Raman spectrum (see Figs. S1(a) and (b)) confirm the successful synthesis of CNDs. From the typical diffraction peak located around 27.0° that is also in good agreement with previous reports [39], one can estimate that the mean crystal size of the CNDs is about 2.60 nm based on the Debye-Scherrer formula. The characteristic vibration modes around 1348 and 1548 cm−1 assigned to CNDs can be observed in the Raman spectrum. In addition, TEM characterization shown in Fig. S1(c) indicates the relatively uniform particle size for the CNDs, and the average size is about 2.58 nm from random sampling of 100 particles (see Fig. S1(d)), which is consistent with the value estimated from the XRD characterization.

Fig. 1 (a) shows the XRD patterns of the CsPbI2Br layers with varying quantities of CND addition to 1.5 wt%. It is evident that the addition of CNDs has no notable influence on the crystal phase of CsPbI2Br [40]. Moreover, the intensity of the main diffraction peak from the (100) crystal plane is enhanced for the CsPbI2Br film with 1.0 wt% CND addition (see Fig. 1(b)), indicating the improved crystallization. Fig. 1(c) exhibits the absorption spectra of the pristine and CND-added CsPbI2Br films deposited on the SnO2-coated ITO glass substrates. Except the limited absorption enhancement around 400 nm originating from CNDs (see the transmission spectrum in Fig. S2), influence of CND addition on light absorption of the CsPbI2Br layers can be neglected. Furthermore, the optical bandgap (E opt) values estimated from the Tauc plot of (αhv)2 versus hv (see Fig. 1(d)) that was derived from the corresponding absorption spectra [41] indicate that CND addition to 1.5 wt% has no notable influence on E opt of the CsPbI2Br films.

To ascertain the impacts of CND addition on the morphology of the CsPbI2Br films, SEM and AFM characterizations were conducted and compiled in Fig. 2 . As indicated by the SEM images, fully covered pinhole-free CsPbI2Br films were obtained for both the pristine and CND-added CsPbI2Br films, demonstrating that CND-addition to 1.5 wt% has no notable influence on the continuity and uniformity of the CsPbI2Br films. The three-dimensional AFM images further reveal that compared with the pristine CsPbI2Br film, appropriate addition of CNDs can significantly reduce the surface roughness of the CsPbI2Br films. As marked in the corresponding AFM images, the root-mean-square (RMS) value of the surface roughness for the 1.0 wt% CND-added CsPbI2Br film is about 17.76 nm, much.

smaller than the value of 26.31 nm for the surface roughness of the pristine CsPbI2Br film. The smaller surface roughness is beneficial to form the better contact with the HTLs, thus mitigating recombination at the corresponding interface and then leading to the better device performance. Furthermore, it is worth noting that the RMS value of the surface roughness has no evident change with the varying CND addition from 0.5 to 1.5 wt%.

Fig. 3 (a) and (b) show the cross-sectional SEM images of the pristine and 1.0 wt% CND-added CsPbI2Br films on ITO glass. The similar interface between the CsPbI2Br and ITO layers for both cases can be observed, indicating the neglectable influence on the interface by the CND addition. Moreover, the thickness of the CsPbI2Br layer is not evidently affected by the CND addition.

Fig. 4 (a) schematizes the device configuration of ITO/SnO2/(CND-added) CsPbI2Br perovskite/P3HT/Ag investigated in this study, and the cross-sectional SEM image for the solar cell with 1.0 wt% CND addition in the CsPbI2Br layer is shown in Fig. 4(b). The layered structure with no voids in between the adjacent layers can be clearly observed, which is a prerequisite for guaranteeing high device performance. Fig. 4(c) presents the energy level diagram of each component [9,42], and the suitable conduction band alignment between CNDs and CsPbI2Br can facilitate transport of the photogenerated electrons across the photoactive layer. The illuminated J-V curves of the PSCs with different addition quantities of CNDs recorded under AM 1.5G are compiled in Fig. 4(d), and the key parameters including PCE, short-circuit current density (J sc), open-circuit voltage (V oc) and fill factor (FF) extracted from the J-V curves are summarized in Table 1 . Compared with the pristine CsPbI2Br PSC, the ones with the CND addition deliver the enhanced performance. Moreover, the 1.0 wt% CND addition is an optimal value for this study.

To test the reproducibility and reliability of performance improvement, 9 more independent devices for each condition were fabricated, and the corresponding PCEs together with the value discussed in Fig. 4(d) are compiled in Fig. 4(f) and Fig. S3, and the key device parameters of PCE, J sc, V oc and FF are summarized in Tables S1–4. One can observe the systematic change trend of the PCE with the CND addition quantity that is fully consistent to that discussed in Fig. 4(d) and Table 1. Moreover, the change of J sc, V oc and FF (see Fig. S3) also confirms the reproducibility and reliability of performance improvement with the CND addition. Under the optimal CND addition, i.e., 1.0 wt%, the highest PCE of 14.69 % can be achieved with the average PCE of 13.77 % for the tested 10 independent devices.

To check the operation stability, time-dependent J for the 1.0 wt% CND-added device was recorded and compared with that of the control device under the applied voltage closed to the maximum power output point and the AM 1.5G illumination. As shown in Fig. 4(f), the CND-added device exhibits more stable current output than the control device.

To understand the mechanisms responsible for the improved device performance because of the CND addition, more investigations were carried out. Fig. 5 (a) compares the steady-state PL spectra of the pristine and 1.0 wt% CND-added CsPbI2Br layers on SnO2-coated ITO glass. It is evident that the intensity of the peak located around 650 nm, corresponding to the bandgap energy of CsPbI2Br, remarkably reduces for the CND-added sample. According to the previous reports [43], it can be ascribed to the transfer of the photoelectrons from CsPbI2Br to the embedded CNDs benefiting from the suitable energy level alignment (see Fig. 4(c)), leading to the unmatched numbers for the electrons in the conduction band and the holes in the valence band, and thus to the reduced PL emission. Accordingly, the enhanced transfer of the photoelectrons across the photoactive layer through the CND “bridges” can reduce the carrier loss because of various recombination mechanisms and thus increase J sc of the related devices [44,45].

Analysis of the space-charge-limited current (SCLC) can be employed to estimate the defect density in the active layers. Fig. 5(b) shows the relationship between the dark current (I) and applied voltage (V) for the pristine and 1.0 wt% CND-added cases with the measurement configuration of ITO/SnO2/CsPbI2Br/PCBM/Ag (see the inset of Fig. 5(b)). For each curve, two distinct regions can be observed, i.e., the linear I
V relation at the low bias and nonlinear increase of I with V at the relatively high voltage, which correspond to the ohmic response and conducting mechanism with all the available trap states filled by the injected carriers, respectively. The voltage corresponding to the intersect point of the tangent lines of the I-V curves in these two regions is called the trap-filled limited voltage, V TFL, which can be employed to estimate the trap density (n t) following the equation of n t = 2ε V TFL/(eL 2), where ε and L are the dielectric constant and the thickness of the CsPbI2Br layer, and e is the elementary charge [46]. The calculated n t for the pristine and 1.0 wt% CND-added CsPbI2Br layers is about 1.02 × 1016 and 7.90 × 1015 cm−3, indicating that CND addition can reduce the trap density in the CsPbI2Br layer.

EIS is another effective tool to reveal the carrier transport behaviors and recombination processes in PSCs. Fig. 5(c) shows the Nyquist plots of the solar cells with and without 1.0 wt% CND addition in dark at the bias of 1.0 V over the frequency from 1 to 1 × 106 Hz and the equivalent circuit is exhibited in the inset. The radius of the semicircle represents the overall recombination resistance (R rec), and the intersect of the EIS curve with the Z′ axis indicates the equivalent series resistance (R s) [47–49]. One notes that the PSC with 1.0 wt% CND addition delivers a higher R rec (For more data, one can refer to Fig. 5(d) and Fig. S4) and smaller R s than the pristine one. During the dark impedance measurement, because the carries are injected only from an external voltage source, the higher R rec thus can be attributed to the reduced trap density in the CsPbI2Br layer, as well as the improved interfacial contact/reduced defects between the photoactive layer and P3HT HTL because of the smaller surface roughness of the CsPbI2Br layer for the CND-added device, as discussed above in Figs. 5(b) and Fig. 2(c′). In the meantime, the improved interfaces between the CsPbI2Br layer and the adjacent layers due to the CND addition-induced surface roughness reduction is beneficial to decrease R s.

To comprehensively characterize the contribution of CND addition on device performance, stability test in air with the humidity of 20–30 % was conducted. Fig. 6 (a) shows the time-dependent change of the normalized PCEs for the pristine and 1.0 wt% CND-added PSCs (More information regarding time-dependent J sc, V oc and FF can be found in Fig. S5). After the measurement at the 80th hour, the pristine device is fully invalid before the next measurement at the 120th hour. In distinct contrast, the CND-added PSC can maintain ∼86 % of the initial PCE even after 160 h store in air with the humidity of 20–30 %. The photographs of the pristine and 1.0 wt% CND-added CsPbI2Br layers on SnO2-coated ITO glass before and after store in air with the humidity of 20–30 % for 50 days (see Fig. S6) indicate that more severe phase change occurs for the pristine layer. To understand the improved stability for the CND-added CsPbI2Br layer, the contact angle was measured. As demonstrated in Fig. 6(b) and (c), one can see that the CND-added CsPbI2Br layer has a bigger contact angle with the water droplet, thus exhibiting the improved moisture resistance and stability, as compared with the pristine case [50].

In this paper, addition of CNDs in the CsPbI2Br layer to improve the performance of the related PSCs is investigated. Thanks to the reduced defect density and surface roughness, as well as the improved crystallinity and moisture resistance for the CsPbI2Br layers with appropriate CND addition, the PSCs deliver the enhanced comprehensive performance including PCE and stability, as compared to the control device without CND addition. Given the facile experimental scheme and green process, it is believed that this study provides valuable exploration for developing the CsPbI2Br-based perovskite solar cells with improved comprehensive performance.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors greatly appreciate the financial support from the Natural Science Foundation of Gansu (Grant No. 20JR10RA611).

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yanzhou Wang is currently a doctoral candidate in School of Physical Science and Technology, Lanzhou University. Her research interests include organic solar cell and perovskite solar cell.

Qiming Liu received his BS and MS degrees in Physics from Lanzhou University China, in 2008 and 2011, respectively. After that, he pursued his PhD degrees, under financial support of Japanese Government Scholarship, in Materials Science from Graduate School of Science and Engineering in Saitama University, Japan, in 2015. Then he stayed in Saitama University as a project researcher for two years. Currently, he is an associate professor in institute of electronic materials of Lanzhou University, China. His research focuses on the use of an integrated approach combining materials, interface, device, and process engineering to improve the energy devices especially in the filed of photovoltaics and rechargeable batteries.

Junshuai Li is currently a Professor at School of Materials & Energy, Lanzhou University, China. Prior to holding this position, he performed the research on preparation of high quality PV materials and structures, design and fabrication of advanced Si nanostructure-based PV devices at Saitama University, Japan (2006.10–2008.09) and Nanyang Technological University, Singapore (2008.09–2012.03). Now his main research interest focuses on the optical and electrical behaviors in subwavelength semiconductor structures, and renewable energy devices

Yali Li received her Bachelor and Master Degrees of Science both from Lanzhou University, China and Doctor Degree of Engineering from Saitama University, Japan. From 2009.01 to 2012.03, she was with Nanyang Technological University, Singapore as a Research Fellow. Now, she holds a faculty position at School of Materials & Energy, Lanzhou University. Her current research focuses on energy-harvesting and storage devices.
